prd.summary: CampusHub consolidates campus events and study-group matching for commuter students. MVP tests whether commuters will adopt an all-in-one app over single-purpose tools.
prd.problem_statement: Commuter students today learn about campus events through scattered channels (email, flyers, multiple apps) and often miss them entirely; 31 of 40 surveyed commuters reported hearing about events after they've ended. Finding study partners requires manual coordination or sketchy off-campus groups. This fragmentation reduces campus engagement and social connection for students who leave campus early.

target_user.who: Commuter students at large public universities who spend 6+ hours on campus per week
target_user.situation: Between classes or during breaks, looking for events, study partners, or campus resources but unaware of what's happening or how to find peers with matching schedules

in_scope.item: Personalized events feed (campus-wide calendar aggregation + push notifications)
in_scope.rationale: Directly addresses the stated pain: 78% of surveyed users miss events due to fragmentation. Tests whether students will open a new app for discovery. Smallest surface to validate adoption.

in_scope.item: Study-group matching by course and schedule
in_scope.rationale: Second-highest friction point; requires no marketplace infrastructure or moderation. Validates the core assumption that students will switch apps for convenience. Low operational overhead for a 2–3 person team.

in_scope.item: Basic user onboarding and course enrollment flow
in_scope.rationale: Required to power study matching and personalization. Establishes the foundation for future features without expanding scope.

out_of_scope.reason: Requires vendor management, payment processing, and dispute resolution. A 2–3 person team cannot ship, moderate, and support a marketplace in a quarter while building core features.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: Marketplace adoption reaches 10% of active users and team grows to 4+

out_of_scope.reason: Requires restaurant integrations, logistics coordination, and real-time inventory. Outside core competency; existing services (DoorDash, Uber Eats) already solve this for campus.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: Events and study matching show 40%+ weekly retention and food vendors request integration

out_of_scope.item: Anonymous forum for questions and confessions
out_of_scope.reason: Requires content moderation at scale and community management. Deferred to avoid distraction from adoption metrics and to prevent moderation burden on small team.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: Core features reach 20% of target user base and moderation SOP is documented

success_metrics.metric: Weekly active commuter students at pilot campus
Week 4 repeat-use rate (students active in weeks 1 and 4)
Events discovered via CampusHub that users attend

risks.risk: Commuters may not open a new app if existing single-purpose tools (Facebook, GroupMe, Eventbrite) already serve their needs.
risks.mitigation: Launch with push notifications for events matching user interests; measure week-1 vs. week-4 retention to detect early churn. If week-4 retention <30%, pivot to a web-first or SMS-first distribution model.

risks.risk: Study-group matching fails if course enrollment data is incomplete or students don't input their schedules.
risks.mitigation: Require course enrollment during onboarding (pull from university registrar API if available; fall back to manual entry). A/B test incentives (e.g., 'complete your schedule to unlock matches') to drive completion.

risks.risk: Pilot campus selection bias: if chosen campus is unusually engaged or tech-forward, results won't generalize.
risks.mitigation: Select a campus with below-median student tech adoption and above-median commuter population. Document campus demographics and compare to national averages before scaling.

open_questions: Riskiest assumption (proposed): Will commuters actually switch from single-purpose apps they already use to an all-in-one, or will CampusHub become a secondary app they check once per week?
Does the target university have a registrar API or student directory we can integrate for course enrollment, or must we rely on manual entry?
What is the baseline weekly active rate for existing campus apps (e.g., official university app, GroupMe) at the pilot campus?
next_release: Used textbook and furniture marketplace (deferred pending 10% marketplace adoption and team growth)
Campus food pickup and delivery integration (deferred pending 40% retention and vendor requests)
Anonymous forum and community Q&A (deferred pending 20% user base and moderation SOP)

flags.issue: Primary metric measures adoption, not engagement or habit formation.
flags.why_it_matters: 25% weekly active by end of Q1 can be driven by novelty; if week-4 retention is 20%, the feature is failing even if the headline metric hits.
flags.suggested_fix: Keep the 25% target as primary, but add a mandatory guardrail: week-4 repeat-use rate ≥40%, or the feature is considered at-risk regardless of WAU.

flags.issue: Evidence is self-selected (your old commuter group chat), not representative.
flags.why_it_matters: 31 of 40 respondents may skew toward students already engaged in campus community; silent majority of commuters may not care about events at all.
flags.suggested_fix: Before launch, survey a random sample of 100+ commuters at the pilot campus on event awareness and study-partner friction; use that to validate or revise the problem.

flags.issue: iOS-only launch is a hard constraint, not a choice, but the PRD doesn't state it.
flags.why_it_matters: Commuters may use Android; launching on one platform risks excluding a segment of the target user and inflating churn if they can't access the app.
flags.suggested_fix: Explicitly commit to iOS-only for MVP with a clear Android deferral trigger (e.g., 'if iOS retention >35% by week 8, ship Android in Q2'). Measure Android demand during pilot.

flags.issue: Target user is broad ('commuter students at large public universities'); no geographic or demographic narrowing.
flags.why_it_matters: Commuter behavior varies widely by region, campus size, and socioeconomic factors; a pilot at one campus may not predict success at another.
flags.suggested_fix: Narrow the target to 'commuter students at [specific campus] with 6+ hours on campus per week' for the MVP, and document why that campus was chosen (e.g., high commuter %, below-median tech adoption).

critique.strongest_element: The riskiest assumption is explicit and the MVP is designed to test it: events + study matching directly address the fragmentation pain, and the deferral of marketplace and food delivery shows discipline in cutting scope to fit a 2–3 person team in a quarter.
critique.weakest_element: The success metric conflates adoption with retention; 25% WAU is a vanity metric if week-4 repeat use collapses, and the intake provides no baseline for what 'good' looks like at a comparable campus.
